text: The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the duodenum, exposing the inferior vena cava (IVC) and renal vein. The dissection proceeds laterally to the IVC, revealing the psoas muscle beneath Girauda's fascia, with the gonadal vein and ureter visible over the psoas. The ureter is circumferentially undermined and clipped with two Hem-o-lok clips for oncologic control. The renal dissection continues with the kidney under tension, moving superiorly along the IVC to reach the renal hilum. The renal artery and vein are dissected free and ligated using a laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the superior aspect of the right kidney while sparing the adrenal gland. The lateral and superior attachments remain intact to aid in traction during the ureteral dissection. Regional lymphadenectomy, including paracaval and intraaorticaval dissection, is performed.

The surgeon places the ureter on tension and dissects it along its length to the bladder, marked by the obliterated umbilical artery. The bladder is mobilized anteriorly for exposure, and entered superior to the ureteral orifice using electrocautery. The ureteral orifice is identified, and dissection is carried circumferentially to create a small bladder cuff. A V-lock absorbable suture is placed at the inferior apex for tension and closure aid. The bladder cuff is excised and closed with full thickness V-lock absorbable sutures bidirectionally, tied in the middle.

The surgeon fills the bladder with 120 to 180 cc of sterile water or saline to check for leaks at the bladder cuff. The peritoneal reflection is sutured closed, and the remaining lateral attachments of the kidney are dissected free.
